Griffith C.J., Barton and O'Connor JJ HOLDEN AND ANOTHER . cS i . APPELL PLAINTIFFS, AND BLACK AND OTHERS . A ' 4 . , RES DEFENDANTS, y Appeat from the Supreme Court of Victoria. HIGH COURT [HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA,]} _ ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF VICTORIA. administrator—Duty of administrator to invest and secure shares of Action quia timet—Covenant to administer—Acceptance of money p Court—Parties— Costs.

The duty of an administrator of an intestate estate to invest and shares'of next of kin who are infants, is analogous to the obligation | next of kin who are sui juris. . action against the administrator as principal debtor, and the next of creditors, in the same way as in the case of other sureties for the p money, when the time for payment has arrived. Mathews v, Saurin, 31 L.R. Ir., 181, followed.

Neither the fact that the sureties have taken a covenant from the trator to duly administer according to law, nor that the sureties, in a against the administrator for breach of duty and on the covena accepted money paid into Court in satisfaction of past breaches, dept sureties of their right to this specific relief. s In such an action by sureties against the administrator and infant kin, the Court may properly order the administrator to set aside a1 the shares of infants in the personalty, direct accounts and inquiries purpose of ascertaining the amounts of those shares, and direct the trator to keep and file proper accounts of the estate. 'The infants' costs of such an action should be paid by the plaintiffs, without an order that they be recovered over against the admin . Semble, in such an action the Court has no jurisdiction to order the any of the parties to be paid out of the estate. Judgment of the Full Court, Holden v.

Black, V.L.R., 3265 205, reversed ; judgment of «Beckett J., restored with variations as OF AUSTRALIA. id Black, a farmer, died on 28th July, 1897, intestate, him surviving his widow, Kate Black, and three infant 'st September, 1897, administration was granted to the and the plaintiffs, James Holden and David Webster, ame sureties for her proper administration of the estate, and p her executed an administration bond in the ordinary form esum of £2766 15s. d discharge the liabilities of the estate ; that she would deposit title deeds of the lands in the name of the deceased and such er deeds as might be issued in substitution thereof in the ak of New South Wales, Benalla, in the joint names of herself, den and Webster, to be held on their joint account until the was fully administered ; that all dealings relating to the deeds or lands should be effected only after obtaining the tho 'ity of Mrs. Black, and Messrs. Holden and Webster ; that uld at all times until the estate was fully administered ilin all respects the conditions of the administration bond, d, in case of any breach thereof, would, out of her share of the e, indemnify Messrs. Holden and Webster from any loss aing thereby ; and that any business of any kind in connection e estate requiring legal skill should be done by Mr. Gilbert , of Benalla, or any other solicitor as might be acting for estate for the time being named by Messrs.

Holden and Letters of administration were issued to Mrs. Black 4th May, 1898. action was brought by Messrs. Holden and Webster against Black and the three infant children, and by the statement of im it was alleged that Mrs.

Black had been guilty of certain waches of trust. and neglect of her duty as administratrix, and hes of her covenant of 23rd April, 1898; that, by reason Black's conduct, the plaintiffs had already been occasioned rable loss and expense in endeavouring to procure Mrs. to remedy such breaches of duty, and to prevent the connce thereof, and to secure themselves against loss or damage H.C. or A. 1905. Houpen Brack. H. C. or A. 1905.

HoLven Brack. HIGH COURT therefrom; and that, unless there should be granted son e relief as was claimed, the estate would not be adm according to law, the estate and the interests therein improper risks, the plaintiffs as sureties would continue t under a personal risk of loss and injury through continue repeated breaches by Mrs. Black of her duties as such adn tratrix and of her covenant with the plaintiffs, and such loss injury, unless prevented or restrained, would be irreparable. — The plaintiffs claimed :— f "(a) A declaration that the defendant Kate Black has administered, and is not administering, the estate of the in in accordance with law, and has been guilty of breaches of! duty as administratrix and of her covenant with the plaintiffs. "(b) An injunction restraining the continuance by the s defendant of such breaches of duty and breaches of covenant "(c) £45 damages from the said defendant in respect of sue breaches of covenant as already committed as aforesaid. " And also such relief of the nature hereinafter specified a the Court may seem proper to secure the proper admini of the said estate, or to remedy or prevent the wrong or hereinbefore complained of, that is to say :— "(d) Administration of the said estate by or under the di of the Court. "(e) The appointment of a receiver or of a person to dischar the duties of administrator or trustee of the said estate in pla the said defendant Kate Black. "(f) Or such other declarations orders or directions as to Court may seem sufficient or proper for the protection of t plaintiffs from any liability to the infant defendants in respect such matters as aforesaid." By her defence, the defendant Mrs.

Black denied the breaches of her duty as administratrix and of the covenant, (paragraph 13) said that, if she had committed any breach o covenant, the plaintiffs had suffered no loss or injury th and were entitled to nominal damages only in respect the and that she brought into Court the sum of one shilling, and that the same was sufficient to satisfy the plaintiffs' respect of any such breach. J OF AUSTRALIA. and treating it as pleading a payment into Court of the 'of one shilling in respect of so much only of their claim as damages for breach of the covenant entered into by this fendant, they say that they accept such sum in satisfaction of im in respect of which it is so paid in. The infant defendants submitted their rights and interests to otection of the Court.

The action was tried before dBeckett J and the facts proved \d found are sufficiently set out in the judgment of Griffith CJ., eunder. ett J. gave judgment according to the following efor the benetit of the defendants, declare as follows :-— (a) That the carrying on of the farming business by the said fendant was beneficial to the estate of the intestate, and that accounts should be directed in respect thereof. (b) That the house in Benalla purchased by the said defendant ld be deemed to form part of the intestate's estate, subject to rection hereinafter contained with respect thereto. "(c) That no accounts should be taken in respect of past alings with the estate, and that the infant defendants having en properly maintained and educated, all income to which the fant defendants were entitled up to the date of this judgment, jould be deemed to have been properly applied by the defendant e Black. (d) The defendant Kate Black not seeking any inquiries as to @ moneys of her own not derived from the estate of the intestate into the bank account kept by her after the intestate's death, moneys which have been paid into the said account shall be dered as belonging to the estate of the said intestate. () All investments made by the said Kate Black since the of the intestate shall be deemed to have been investments noney of the estate. = 1905. coed Hoven cA Buacr. In their reply the plaintiffs said :—As to paragraph 13 of the H.C. or A. H.C. or A. 1905.

Sew, HoLpEen v. Buack. HIGH COURT "(f) The said Kate Black is not entitled to any reward in form of commission or of salary for her management o 7 business which was carried on by her. f "(g) Should any loss of the estate arise by reason of security given by Murray being a deficient security for the : of £450 advanced thereon, the defendant Kate Black shall. personally liable to make good the loss arising from lendin such insufficient security. " And this Court doth order that on the distribution of the e of the intestate, as between the defendant Kate Black and t infant defendants, she shall take as part of her share, at the pri of £260, the house at Benalla purchased by her. " Order that the defendant Kate Black, in addition to any o accounts which may be required to show her dealings with | corpus of the estate, do keep a proper account of all sums he after to be received, as income of the trust estate and all disbu ments chargeable against such income, so that the net amoun the two-thirds share of the income to which the infants will entitled may be ascertained therefrom, and that she do permit plaintiffs, or any proper person authorized by them, from time: time to inspect such accounts. "Refer to tax the costs of the plaintiffs and of the defend: including discovery. Order the defendant Kate Black to pay plaintiffs' costs and the infant defendants' costs and retain own costs out of the estate of the intestate. Liberty to apply On appeal to the Full Court (Madden C.J., Holroyd and Ho JJ.,) judgment was ordered to be entered for the defendants wil costs: Holden v.
Black (1). The plaintifis now appealed to the High Court. Duffy K.C., and Weigall, for the appellants.

This is not administration suit, but an action by which the plaintiffs seek be protected, and, if administration be in the opinion of Court the proper remedy, the plaintiffs are willing that its be ordered. The plaintiffs however do not desire an adniinis ' (Grirrirx C.J.—Do the plaintiffs want an injunction now?] They want what the Court thinks is the proper remedy, x (1) V.L.R., 326; 26 A.L.T., 205. OF AUSTRALIA. sare entitled to relief under the circumstances of this case. ourt in its equitable jurisdiction will interpose before any injury has been suffered on the principle of a bill quia eg., a surety may compel the debtor on a bond in which joined to pay the debt when due, whether the surety has tually sued for it or not, and a covenantee may obtain ief in similar circumstances: Mitford on Pleading, p. 171, d with approval in Wooldridge v.

Norris (1). See also 's Equity Jurisprudence, 1st English ed., secs. 825, 827 ; ed., sees. 730, 850; Lindley on Partnership, 6th ed., p. 378. re y may put the creditor in motion against the debtor, and, he creditor does an act by which he is prevented from plying with the request of the surety, the surety is discharged. vton v Chorlton (2). The creditors in this case being infants, only method of obtaining relief is by coming to the Court, and nging the infants there as well.

The reason of this principle "it is unreasonable that a man should always have a cloud ging over him:" Snell's Principles of Equity, 11th ed., 504. also Antrobus v. Davidson (3); Hobbs v. Wayet (4); Becherse vy.
Hayes (8); Hughes-Hallett v. Indian mmoth Gold Mines Co. (9). It is the duty of a surety to look rhis principal : In re Wakefield (10); In re Carpenter (11); In Lockey (12); Wright v.

Simpson (13). The Court is at liberty to 'the relief in any way it thinks fit. An entirely different ly is open to the appellants on the covenant, but they do quire it if they are sufficiently protected as sureties.

If the ts are not entitled to succeed as sureties, they are entitled have the bond carried out: Wooldridge v. Norris (1). The nt into Court has nothing to do with the right of the nts to bring an action quia timet. uR., 6 Eq., 410. (8) 1 Vern., 189.
D., 398. (13) 6 Ves., 714, at p. 734. are satisfied with the judgment of dBeckett J. The appelH. 0. or A. 1905. — HoipEen v Brack. Ti4 H.C. oF A. 1905. > Hoipes »% Brack.

HIGH COURT They also referred to Harris v. Bouts, Cash Chemists (8 Ltd. (1). Isaacs A.G., and Guest, for the respondent Kate Black. the cases cited for the appellants a loss had accrued, and _ was an existing liability upon the surety.
That is clearly in Mathews v. Sawrin (2). Here the consideration of any loss has been eliminated by the acceptance of the money into Court.

The administratrix is liable under the b respect only of the estate as left by the intestate. She liable to the infant respondents as trustee, but with that li the surety is not concerned. There is no duty upon thea tratrix under the bond to keep accounts, although there may b e trustee of the infants.

The only way the children could recov the bond would be by having the bond assigned, and the Court in its discretion refuse to assign the bond unless some actual has been sustained: In ve Carpenter (3); s.c., sub. nom, holland v. Smith (4); In re Steele (5). The issue has taken whether there was or was not any loss, but it was that there was no loss.

That being so, the appellants we entitled to bring their action. In Scott v. Wilson (6) it was he that a surety was not entitled to an order for administral though the administrator had not administered for many y The surety upon entering into the bond must look only to administrator.

He has no right to sue the beneficiaries, and hi is no relation between him and them. (Grirrira C.J.—There is the ordinary relation of eureiya creditor between them.] ' But the beneficiaries have nothing to do with the bond. surety is neither a creditor nor a legatee, and therefore is n entited to bring this administration action: Subraya Chet A. S. Rajammal (7).

The mere fact that the administrator k land unsold during the infancy of the beneficiaries gives no to the surety to sue. The trusts which the administrator takes are contained in see. 8 of the Administration and P (1) 2 Ch., 376. (5) 23 V.L.R., 146; 19 A.L.T., 1 (2) 31 LR. 81, at p. 187. (6) 2 Q.L.d., 26. (3) 20 V.L.R., 15! (7) 14 Madras L.J. R., 482. (4) 20 V.L.R., 403.
As to the duties of an administrator, see sv. Brain (1). The administration lasts until the estate is nistered, and the surety is liable until then.

There is no jon upon the administrator to administer within any lar time: Story's Equity Jurisprudence, 1st English ed., . 327, 639. The only statutory provision as to the time of ent is that the administrator cannot be called on to pay ing until a year has elapsed since the grant of administra». The administrator cannot get a discharge from infants, ept by paying their money into Court, and he must wait until y are of age: Williams on Executors, 10th ed., p. 1137; v.

Thornton (2); Rotheram v. Fanshaw (3). An executor dministrator has been compelled to secure the shares of ieficiaries where he was on the brink of insolvency, and there olution of Real Estate, pp. 329, 332.

But, by not setting aside ecuring the shares of infants, an administrator does not commit reach of the bond, although he may be guilty of a breach of duty as trustee: Pitt v. Woodham (4). That case emphasises proposition that the administrator is only liable for the sy of the intestate.

He may have to account for profits as e, but not as administrator. A surety can only sue if there h of duty causing loss, or rendering the danger of loss minent. Ifthe right were larger than that, the surety could e day after the bond was given: Fletcher v.
Bealey (5); ick vy. Stanley (6). The action quia timet lies only where itor has a present right to sue and refuses to do so: 's Principles of Equity, 11th ed., p. 583; Thomson's Comum, p. 572.
In s v. Davidson (8), as in the present case, it was uncertain er there was any loss. The surety must be able to say what um ount of his liability is.
The case of Wooldridge v. Norris (5) 28 Ch. D., 688. (6) 9 Ha., 627. (7) 1 Vern,, 189. (8) 3 Mer., 569. simminent danger of loss.
See also Robbins and Maw on the | 890, referring back to the Statute of Distributions (22 & 23 H.C. or A. 1905. —— Hoven v Brack. H. C. or A. 1905. a Hoipen v Brack.

HIGH COURT (1) turns on the construction of a particular covenant. The will not grant redress to a surety except in respect of p liability. There must be some liability defined and ascertain Ex parte Snowdon (2).
In Hughes-Hallett v. Indian Mami Gold Mines Co. (3), there was no imminent danger of loss, and redress was given. On the other hand, in Hobbs v.
Wayet (4), Wolmershausen vy, Gullick (5), where there was imminent dange of loss redress was given. See also Campbell v. Macomb (6); re Lockey (7); Re Denekin, Peters v.

Sawrin (10) is in this particular liability—an actual accrued debt—on his part to ereditor. Although the appellants might have been entitled their costs up to the time of payment into Court, if they stopped there, having gone on with an unfounded claim, it within the discretion of the Court whether they should get e' those costs. The covenant to indemnify does not add anythin; to the appellants' rights except that Mrs.

Black has by it pledg her one-third share of the estate. [They also referred to Wis v. Smith (11); Underhill on Trusts, 6th ed., p. 871; Lewin Trusts, 11th ed., p. 1134; Trusts Act 1896, sec. 18.] Vasey and Cohen, for the infant respondents. The infants strangers to this matter as it was launched.

They are not eredi in respect of the administration bond. That bond was given the Court, and the Court will not allow it to be assigned un there is a breach which has caused loss. The action should h been dismissed as against the infants with costs, and under circumstances should they have been made liable for the costs of the appellants. Having been made respondents to the appeal to the Full Court, the infants were entitled to appear and ask have the order as to costs altered: Hast India Co. v.

D., 484, at p. 494. OF AUSTRALIA. o referred to In ve Dean (1); In ve McMillan (2); Re Butters Weigall in reply. The administratrix, not having done her as administratrix, has broken her contract with the sureties. y will then allow the sureties to intervene at once, and nt a continuance of the breach: Rowlatt on Principal and , p. 139.

To the extent of the bond the surety is liable for y breach of duty by the administratrix as to the property of intestate or its increase: Williams on Evxecutors, 10th ed., . IL, p. 1279; Dobbs v. Brain (7).

The duty of the administrix as to the shares of infants is to get those shares ready for istribution. Having done so, she then holds the shares as trustee r the children. But not having got the shares of the infants set she has committed a breach of her bond.

The only effect epting the money paid into Court is that the appellants entitled to have their costs taxed, and go on as to the other ef claimed. It has no effect as to the claim for an injunction. respondent Mrs. Black should have paid the whole of the pellants' costs of the action, and those costs include the costs of » infants.
Mrs. Black could not have appealed as to costs use she was only given liberty to pay her costs out of the Although the infants might have appealed as to costs, lid not. Cur. adv. vult.

The judgment of the Court was read by Guirrira C.J. The following statement of facts is taken from judgment of &Bechett J. :— avid Black, a farmer, died intestate in July, 1897, leaving a dow and three young children, (5) 26 V.L.R. (6) 9 W.R. (7) 2 Q.B., 207, at p. 212, 24; 22 ALL.T., 45. LEU und by an order made in an action like the present. [They H. ©. or A. 1905, Hotpen v.

Brack, October 2 H.C. oF A. 1905. HIGH COURT "The widow obtained administration, and the plaintiffs, a request, and as an act of friendship, became her sureties, — "They at the same time required her to enter into a covenant with them, binding her to certain specified acti 'relation to the estate, and by which she agreed with them fulfil in all respects the conditions of the bond she had ente into as administratrix. "The estate consisted of land valued at £1,562 10s. and p sonalty valued at £1,204, some of which consisted of live s The widow carried on a farming business for about two year "She lent some money belonging to the estate on imp security, but the loan was repaid. "She bought a house in Benalla for £260, when she wound the farming business, in which she and the infants resided. "She did not keep proper accounts of her receipts and p ments. " She did not file her fifteen months' account within the requ period, and, when she did file it, is was made up imperfectly fron the best materials available, and was not a full statement of de ings with the estate. , "She kept only one banking account, into which she paid large sums received, and upon which she drew for all purposes 0 her own or of her children or of the estate. "Her conduct was honest in motive and beneficial in resu but did not fulfil strictly her obligations as administratrix. " About June, 1903, the sureties took alarm from some proe ings against other sureties who had become involved in heavy liabilities, and they sought to make their own position secure _ getting the administratrix to adopt a more regular mode ¢ administration and satisfy them as to past transactions. "One item inserted in her fifteen months' account was which she treated as paid to herself for salary. "They applied to her for information as to this and oth matters but did not obtain any. She referred them to her so itor, and a correspondence began which continued from June November, 1903. "Tt appeared that the item £468 was inserted at the sugge: of the solicitor whom the sureties had asked her to employ, a OF AUSTRALIA. she did not intend to make any claim to it.

Unfortunately + matters were not so satisfactorily disposed of, and in vember the plaintiffs brought an action against the adminisand her children, asking for a declaration that the adminatrix had been guilty of breaches of her duty as administratrix, of her covenant with them, seeking £45 damages, and an junction against further breaches, and administration of the te by the Court so far as might be necessary to secure proper nistration of the estate. ; The administratrix put in a defence denying that she had ted any breaches of trust, and submitting that the statent of claim disclosed no cause of action, or equity to any relief. » paid one shilling into Court in answer to the claim for damShe also offered to take over the house purchased as an ation of part of her share in the estate." he only other facts necessary to be stated are that the plains accepted the shilling in satisfaction of their claim for damages ach of covenant; that none of the land had been sold, and ippropriation had been made of the shares of the infant en in the personalty, nor had those shares been invested. 18 of the Act No. 1421 (Trusts Amendment Act) which lies to administrators, provides that when property is held by s for an infant the trustees may pay the whole or part of income to the infant's parent or guardian, or apply it for the ant's maintenance, education or benefit, and shall accumulate lue at compound interest by investing it in authorized ities. This section manifestly assumes that the property of it, to which its provisions are to apply, has been ascere defendants contended that the action would not lie, but ckett J. thought that, having regard to the deed executed by administratrix, and the irregularities that had occurred, and Position of the plaintiffs as sureties, they were entitled to the aid of the Court to have the position of the estate ned, and to secure the due performance of her duties by ministratrix, although no loss had as yet happened. His accordingly made a series of declarations, such as might be ly made in an administration suit, and the effect of which H.
C. or A. 1905. os HoLpen v. Brack. H.C.or A' may be summarized as a settlement of the accounts 1905. ey Horpes v.
Brack. HIGH COURT administratrix up to the date of the judgment. They i a declaration that a piece of land alleged te have be chased in whole or part with moneys of the estate formed of the estate.

He further ordered the defendant, the adm trix, to keep proper accounts in future, and to permit the pl i to inspect them, and to pay the costs of the other parti liberty to deduct them and to pay her own costs out of the é On appeal to the Full Court this judgment was rev the action was dismissed with costs, but the learned Judges ' not unanimous in their reasons. Madden C.J. thought the a was, first, for damages for breach of covenant, secondly, fo injunction, and, thirdly, for administration of the estate by Court. So far as regarded the claim for damages, he pointed that it was disposed of by the payment into Court and accept of the sum paid in, With regard to the claim for an injune he pointed out that the covenant was an affirmative eovena and he thought that the case was not one in which the © should exercise its discretion by granting an injunction, So as the suit was to be regarded as a suit for administration, said that it had not been contended that sureties to an adm tration bond have a right to have a decree made for administrat of the estate, and he apparently thought that it followed that declarations made by the learned Judge of first instance inappropriate.

Holroyd J. treated the matter as conelud the claim for damages and the acceptance by the plaintiffs of nominal sum paid into Court, but he was strongly inclined to th that, apart from the deed, the plaintiffs would have been en to invoke the aid of the Court. He thought that the a of the sum paid into Court was an acknowledgment that commencement of the action either no loss had acerued to estate for which the administratrix or her sureties wou responsible, or that any such loss had been made good. Heag that the plaintiffs were not justified in instituting an admin tion suit in their own right, or in asking for directions could only be properly granted in such a suit with the object preventing possible future breaches of trust.

But this was he thought the plaintiffs had done, treating the admin I OF AUSTRALIA. Judge had no authority to make a decree which would ome out of the infants' share of the estate. Hood J. concurred ving the appeal, but gave no reasons for his judgment.

No s of the appeal were given to the infants. om this judgment the plaintiffs have appealed to this Court, he infants have given notice of cross-appeal as to their costs the appeal to the Full Court. The plaintiffs did not contend us that sureties to an administration bond are entitled to titute a suit for administration of the estate. But it was tended that they have the same rights as other sureties in ses, and that the facts showed a case entitling them, as to immediate relief against the administratrix.

It was conled also that their claim to relief under the covenant, whether y ay of damages or injunction, was a distinct cause of action, that their right to relief as sureties could not be affected by success or non-success, or the extent of their success, in ect of it. In our opinion this latter contention is well ed, and we cannot help thinking that, if Holroyd J. had ached so much importance to the argument founded on ontrary contention, he would have agreed with the judgment Beckett J. It is necessary, therefore, to consider what are hts of sureties against their principal when the time has for the performance by the principal of the duty of h they have undertaken to guarantee the performance.

The tion of the bond is that the administratrix "shall well and y collect and administer according to law" the property and hereditaments goods chattels and credits of the d which shall come to her possession, and make and within three months at the office of the Master in Equity inventory, and shall within fifteen months make and a true and just account of her administration as to her and disbursements, and as to what portion of the estate d and what portion is uncollected. So far as the lands ML. 1. 53 neillary to the covenant. He thought with regret that the H.C. or A. 1905. —— Houpen v Brack.
H.C. or A. 1905. HoLpEN Brack. HIGH COURT are concerned, it may be taken, for the purposes of this case, they are held upon a discretionary trust for sale.

No qu therefore arises as to the real-estate. But as to the pers¢ estate it is clear that the administratrix had made defaul filing a proper account, and that, although the prescribed ¢ had long passed, she had not set apart or invested the infi shares, although she had realised the whole of the property discharged all the debts. ' There can be no doubt that at the time when this action brought the infants might by their next friend have institut action to have the estate administered and their shares ascertai and set apart and invested. The time had come when this du of the administratrix ought to have been performed, and t obligation to appropriate their shares and make them secure y an existing obligation capable of immediate enforcement, and default in the performance of which the sureties were liable, } can there be any doubt that the administratrix had, under circumstances, become a trustee for the infants, or that th shares were trust money in her hands, or that it was primd fa a breach of trust to keep them in her hands uninvested.

In our opinion, this obligation of the administratrix appropriate and secure the shares was as much an immediate | as the obligation under a bond to pay a specitic sum of money an appointed day. Ifthe next of kin had been sui juris, i clear that no distinction could be drawn between the cases. A we cannot see any sound reason for holding that the fact tha were infants establishes any valid distinction.

The duty administratrix to invest and secure the shares was analogous to the obligation to pay next of kin who are sui This then being the position and duty of the administra what were the rights of the plaintiffs as sureties for her? It is in our opinion established by authority that, when a ( is due and the creditor does not call upon the debtor for pay and compel the latter to make payment of the debt; for, a Keeper North said in Lord Ranelaugh v. Hayes (1): "It unreasonable that a man should have such a cloud (1) 1 Vern., 190.

OF AUSTRALIA. r him." Mitford on Pleading, p. 148; Story's Equity B. 0. or A. dence, 13th ed., vol. I, sec. 845; Story's Equity dence, 1st English ed., sec. 639. In Wolmershausen v. jick (1), all the cases on the subject of the rights of a surety demnity from his principal were reviewed by Wright J.

In t case the creditor was not a party to the action. But the e of Mathews v. Suwrin (2), in which a surety brought his n against the creditor and the principal debtor to compel Jatter to discharge his debt and succeeded, is exactly in point. that case Porter M.R. denied, and we think rightly, the fication supposed to have been suggested by Z'urner V.C. in ick v.

Stanley (3), that the surety could not sue unless the ipal creditor has refused to do so. It follows that in the nt case the plaintiffs were primd facie entitled to bring an ion against the administratrix, as the principal debtor, and the of kin, as soon as the time had arrived at which the shares the latter ought to have been ascertained, and to ask for a gment that the shares should be set apart and secured, so as ieve them from further liability. It is contended, however, by taking the express covenant from the administratrix, or, my rate, by suing upon it and recovering damages for past ches, they disentitled themselves to take this course.

But ly? An express promise by a surety to do what he is by law und to do, may give an additional cause of action, but cannot nish his obligation to do that which he was originally bound lo, and is now doubly bound to do. Nor can the satisfaction im for past breaches discharge a person from a continuing ion.

The objection that an action for administration of the cannot be brought by the sureties is in our judgment ly inconclusive. If the nature of the obligation of the pal debtor is such that the amount which he ought to pay itor, the time for payment having arrived, cannot be ertained without taking an account or making inquiries, the ng of the account and the making of the inquiries are ntal to the right of relief. And the fact that similar or inquiries might be directed in a suit brought by other 8 for different relief is no reason why the surety should 1893) 2 Ch., 514. (2) 31 L.R., Ir., 181. (3) 9 Ha., 627. 1905. ee Honpen Ds Brack. 1905, Hope Brack.

HIGH COURT Lord Cottenham L.C, in Re Lockey (1), suggest that it is the dut of sureties for official administrators to see that their principa perform their duty. They are clearly responsible for their defan and it would bea singular state of the law if they could not enfore the duty for the performance of which they are responsible, a The only other reason that was urged against the plaintiff right to bring their action was that the bond could not be put i suit against them without the leave of the Court, and that the Court would not assign it for that purpose without proof actual loss, which might never arise. But, when the time is p at which the money ought to have been paid or secured, is a default already committed, and the sureties are not bound wait until they are protected by Statutes of Limitation befor taking steps to relieve themselves from the contingent liability, in the hope that their principal will some day perform the obligati which he ought to have performed already.

For these reasons we are of opinion that the action was proper brought by the plaintiffs; and, although the precise form of th claim to relief may have been inexactly stated, this is no rei for denying them such relief as they were entitled to. The learn Judge might, therefore, at the trial have properly ordered defendant, the administratrix, to set aside and secure for th benefit of the infant next of kin the shares of the personalty which they were entitled in distribution, and might have directed all necessary accounts and inquiries for the purpose of ascertaini the amount of the shares. We think that he might further h directed the administratrix, in aceordance with the terms of the bond, to file proper accounts showing the true condition of estate and the investments by which it was represented. judgment actually pronounced did not proceed exactly upon these lines, and, perhaps, did not give the plaintiffs all that they w ' strictly entitled to. But the declarations which it contained v such as it was within the competence of the Court to make in suit to which all the persons beneficially interested were parti The Attorney-General, indeed, disclaimed any desire to objections to the judgment on merely formal grounds, and admit (1) 1 Ph, 509, OF AUSTRALIA. nd the order to keep proper accounts in future was justified by the express covenant and by the obligation to be implied the administration bond.

So far, therefore, as the substance the judgment of &Beckett J. is concerned, we are of opinion at the appeal to the Full Court should have been dismissed. Amore serious difficulty arises with regard to the costs. In t v.

Simpson (1) it is said that relief of the kind sought in suit may be obtained by a surety if he will indemnify the ditor against the expenses of the suit. We do not regard this ietum as establishing a rule of law but rather a principle of aimess. For the creditor is not in default. He has obtained he benefit of a surety to guarantee the debt due by the principal ebtor.

If it is not paid, both the principal and the surety are in default, and one or other or both of them ought primd facie oi ndemnify him against the costs of a litigation not occasioned by any fault of his. In the case of infant creditors the argument not less convincing. Again: «Beckett J. ordered the administo pay the costs of all parties out of the estate, thus ig two-thirds of the costs upon the infants' shares.

In aking this order he was actuated by the consideration that the ction had on the whole been for the benefit of the estate, and he It justitied in following the analogy of the rules adopted in Iministration actions, in which it is common to give costs out of estate. The present, however, is not an administration action, d we doubt the power of the Court to order payment of costs at of a fund which it is not administering. The case of the of probate actions, which are often ordered to be paid out of le personal estate, is anomalous, and the practice ought not, in ir opinion, to be extended to a new class of actions. For both sons we agree with Holroyd J. in his opinion that the costs of he action ought not to have been ordered to be paid out of the e, and we think that this is a matter of principle on which in appeal will lie.

See In re Mills' Estate (2). With regard to the costs of the appeal to the Full Cow are of opinion that, while affirming the judgment on the that the costs of all parties should be paid out of the estate, we think that this in itself was a point of sufficient importa to have justified the administratrix as the protector of t interests of the infants, in appealing from the judgment on tl point. It is true that she did not appeal on that ground, was open on the appeal, and she ought to have succeeded on it which event she would have obtained so substantial a vari of the judgment as to justify an order that the appellants respondents should bear their own costs of the appeal.
